summary: Jeff Atwood argues that developers should commit code to source control frequently rather than waiting until features are complete. He contends that going days without commits creates integration problems and makes code effectively invisible to the team. The post distinguishes between broken code (unacceptable to commit) and incomplete code (perfectly fine to commit as stubs). Atwood proposes a 'software accretion' model where functionality is built up through many small, tested commits rather than large code drops. This approach enables continuous feedback, code review, and integration throughout development.
key_points: Working more than a day without checking in code creates serious integration problems
Code that isn't in source control effectively doesn't exist - it's invisible to the team
There's an important distinction between broken code and incomplete code; stubs and skeletons are valuable to commit
Developers who reject waterfall methodology often unknowingly practice waterfall-style source control
Software accretion - building through tiny, tested, frequent commits - keeps code always functional while growing incrementally
money_quotes: Check in early, check in often.
If the code isn't checked into source control, it doesn't exist.
I'd much rather have small fragments checked in periodically than to go long periods with no idea whatsoever what my coworkers are writing.
I'm not proposing developers check in broken code – but I also argue that there's a big difference between broken code and incomplete code.

Developers that wouldn't even consider adopting the old-school waterfall method of software development somehow have no problem adopting essentially the very same model when it comes to their source control habits.
Start with a tiny fragment of code that does almost nothing. Look on the bright side – code that does nothing can't have many bugs!
You always have functional software. It may not do much, but it runs.
key_insight: Frequent small commits aren't just about backup—they make your work visible, enable collaboration, and paradoxically reduce risk by keeping code always functional.
